text: Welcome to another episode of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're going to be reviewing one of my favorite applications of robotics and that's in the repair of lumbar hernia. This is a patient who had previously had a lumbar fusion via flank incision who presented with an incisional hernia. She had minimal abdominal surgical history and was a good candidate for robotic repair. Review of cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a hernia that measured eight by seven centimeters with an EHSL or defect. The hernia involved a call in retroperitoneum and there seemed to be some preservation of the external abdominal oblique aponeurosis.

The patient is positioned in the cubitus position for optimal access to the psoas muscle. The surgeon performs a left-sided docking for a transabdominal preperitoneal pre-transversalis repair. Limited adhesions around the hernia site are removed, and the colon is drawn up with the peritoneum without mobilization to avoid damaging the peritoneal flap. The peritoneal dissection begins easily, aided by the healthy preperitoneal fat pad in the lateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs subdiaphragmatic dissection above and below the hernia defect, working around the transversus abdominis and quadratus lumborum. Retroperitoneal fat is reduced and reflected off the pseudosac, which consists of the external abdominal oblique aponeurosis. The dissection extends beyond the lateral edge of the hernia to the psoas muscle and diaphragm.

The surgeon highlights the likely position of the iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal nerves before starting fascial closure. Two separate sutures are used: one for the anterior layer and another for the deeper layer, employing an eighteen-inch V-lock suture to draw the tissue together. The anterior plication visibly offloads tension and reduces dead space, changing the morphology of the hernia and approximating the edges closely.

The surgeon completes the anterior layer placation and proceeds with closure of the deeper myofascial layers using a canal style stitch, integrating muscle into the suture bites. The canal stitch is tightened by alternating the pull direction to prevent tissue tearing. Once the tissue is re-approximated, a baseball style stitch is used to run the suture back on itself, fully reconstructing the sidewall. The circumferential dissection is exposed, preparing the area for mesh placement.

The surgeon places a 25x25 heavyweight mesh over the hernia site, securing it medially with stitches to the psoas muscle. The mesh is unrolled to cover the sidewall and flank, with approximately ten to fifteen fixation stitches using fast-absorbing multifilament suture. The peritoneal flap is then closed, and preperitoneal air is evacuated through a 15mm port.
